prs.title: docs: bump minimum Node version to 20.12.0 (#2394)
prs.body:
Fixes #2394.

As reported, `@iconify/utils` now requires `node:util.styleText`, which was introduced in `Node v20.12.0`. This PR updates the engines field across all packages and reflects the new minimum requirement in the `README` and `Getting Started guide` to prevent build crashes for users on older Node versions.

prs.title: Fix InlinePanel min_num validation with default values

labels.name: status:Needs Review
labels.description: Ready for review by maintainers, awaiting code review, pending feedback

labels.name: component:Panels
labels.description: Edit interface panels, field and structured panels, edit handlers

prs.body:
## Description

Fixes #13546

InlinePanel with `min_num=1` was incorrectly failing validation when saving unchanged forms that contain default values. The error "Submit at least 1 form" would appear even though the required form was displayed with default values.

## Root Cause

Django's formset validation uses `has_changed()` to determine if a form was "submitted". Forms with only default values that weren't edited return `has_changed()=False`, causing them to not count toward the `min_num` requirement.

## Solution

- Created `WagtailBaseChildFormSet` that extends `BaseChildFormSet`
- Overrode `clean()` method to properly handle forms with default values
- Added `_form_has_default_values()` helper method to detect forms with non-empty defaults
- Configured `WagtailAdminModelFormMetaclass` to use the custom formset

## How It Works

1. When validating, the formset checks if forms have default values
2. Forms with defaults are counted as valid submissions (not just forms where `has_changed()` returns True)
3. If enough valid forms exist, validation passes without the error
4. Normal validation behavior is preserved when formsets are truly empty

## Testing

- Added comprehensive regression tests in `test_inline_panel_min_num_defaults.py`
- Tests cover three scenarios:
   - Unchanged forms with default values (the bug fix)
   - Changed values (normal behavior)
   - Truly empty formsets (validation still fails correctly)
- All 2,328 existing admin tests pass
- Manually verified fix works in demo site

## Checklist

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] Tests have been added
- [x] All tests pass locally
- [x] Code follows the project's style guidelines

prs.title: fix(client): preserve schema JSDoc for select/include and resolve TS2536
prs.body:
## Summary

**Fixes a TypeScript issue in Prisma Client where schema comments (JSDoc) were missing from fields queried with select or include. Also resolves TS2536: Type 'K' cannot be used to index type 'S & I' by introducing a safe indexing helper.**


### Root Cause

The mapped type in GetFindResult previously iterated over keyof S | keyof I and directly indexed (S & I)[K].

- This lost access to $ModelPayload['scalars'], dropping JSDoc metadata.
- TypeScript 5.8 introduced stricter checks, making direct indexing invalid.

**Fix**
- Iterate over `keyof P['scalars']` instead of `keyof S | keyof I`, ensuring payload-based metadata is preserved.
- Introduce a helper to safely access intersected keys:
```
type SafeKeyAccess<S, I, K extends PropertyKey> =
  K extends keyof (S & I) ? (S & I)[K] : undefined;

```
- Replace all (S & I)[K] references with SafeKeyAccess<S, I, K>.

**Result**
- IntelliSense now consistently shows schema comments for all selected fields.
- All packages build cleanly (pnpm -r run build).
- No runtime or API surface changes — type-only fix.

### Example

**Before:**

`user2.username // (property) username: string | undefined`


**After:**

`user2.username // (property) username: string | undefined — "user's name"`

**Verification**
- [x] Issue reproduced and validated in sandbox.
- [x] Monorepo builds successfully.
- [x] IntelliSense verified for both findUnique and findUnique(select).

prs.title: Added CRUD APIs for Posts using Prisma and Express
prs.body:
Description:

This PR implements Create, Read, Update, and Delete (CRUD) endpoints for user posts within the /backend folder.

Fixes #15

Key Changes:

  • Added Prisma schema defining User ↔ Post relationship.

  • Implemented controllers for handling CRUD operations (createPost, getPost, updatePost, deletePost).

  • Added Express routes under /routes/posts.js.

  • Integrated JWT-based authorization — only post owners can update or delete their posts.

  • Added basic validation for required fields (title, content).

  • Updated README.md with setup and local testing instructions.

Endpoints:

Method | Endpoint | Description -- | -- | -- POST | /posts/create | Create a new post (requires auth) GET | /posts/:id | Fetch a post by ID PUT | /posts/update/:id | Update a post (only owner) DELETE | /posts/delete/:id | Delete a post (only owner)

Tech Stack:

  • Node.js + Express for backend logic

  • Prisma ORM + MySQL for database layer

  • JWT Authentication for route protection

  • Dotenv for environment configuration

Testing:

Run locally:

cd backend
npx prisma db push
node server.js

Use Postman or Thunder Client to test routes with:

  • Authorization: Bearer <token>

  • JSON body for create/update:

    {
  "title": "My First Post",
  "content": "This is an example post content."
}

Notes:

  • All code changes are scoped under /backend.

  • Comments added for clarity.

  • Ready for review and feedback before final merge.

prs.title: Added Floyd’s Cycle Detection Algorithm in JS (Linked List)
prs.body:
## Description

Solves #117 
Added Cycle Detection in Linked List using Floyd’s Tortoise and Hare Algorithm.
This function efficiently checks if a linked list has a cycle using two pointers moving at different speeds.

File Added: `dsa/linkedlist/detectCycle.js`

### Details:
- Time Complexity: O(n)
- Space Complexity: O(1)

Includes proper comments and clean JS structure (ListNode, hasCycle).

### Why

Enhances the Linked List section by introducing a classic two-pointer DSA problem — frequently asked in interviews and essential for beginners.

### Contributor

Vansh Dagar | [GitHub](https://github.com/GoldFoylee)

prs.title: fix: clarify Step 26 instructions in Shopping Cart workshop

labels.name: scope: curriculum
labels.description: Lessons, Challenges, Projects and other Curricular Content in curriculum directory.

prs.body:
**Description**
Improved clarity of Step 26 instructions in the Shopping Cart workshop to address student confusion about the `currentProductCountSpan` variable and its purpose.

**Changes Made:**
- Add context about span element creation in future steps
- Explain purpose of currentProductCountSpan variable
- Improve clarity for students about element purpose

Fixes #61905

**Checklist:**
<!-- Please follow this checklist and put an x in each of the boxes, like this: [x]. It will ensure that our team takes your pull request seriously. -->
- [x] I have read and followed the [contribution guidelines](https://contribute.freecodecamp.org).
- [x] I have read and followed the [how to open a pull request guide](https://contribute.freecodecamp.org/how-to-open-a-pull-request/).
- [x] My pull request targets the `main` branch of freeCodeCamp.
- [x] I have tested these changes either locally on my machine, or Gitpod.

<!--If your pull request closes a GitHub issue, replace the XXXXX below with the issue number.-->
Closes #61905

<!-- Feel free to add any additional description of changes below this line -->
**Note:** As clarified by the maintainer, the original instructions were technically correct. However, I recognized that students were struggling with understanding the context and purpose of the `currentProductCountSpan` variable. This PR adds minor clarifications to improve the learning experience without changing the technical accuracy of the instructions.

The enhanced explanation helps students understand:
1. Why the variable references a "span" when they're currently working with buttons
2. That this span element will be created in future steps
3. The overall purpose of this preparation step in the workshop flow

This should reduce confusion while maintaining the existing instructional approach.

issues.title: Finance Dashboard – Interactive Expense Tracker with Charts
issues.body:
🪙 Finance Dashboard – Interactive Expense Tracker with Charts

Description
A simple yet visually rich Finance Dashboard built in React to track expenses and visualize spending habits.
Users can log daily expenses, categorize them (Food, Travel, Rent, Utilities, etc.), and view summaries via interactive charts.
The project will use localStorage for data persistence and Recharts / Chart.js for analytics.

🎯 Features

  • ➕ Add new expenses with amount, date, and category

  • 🗂️ Filter expenses by month or category

  • 📊 View visual breakdowns (Pie chart for categories, Bar chart for monthly spending)

  • 💾 Persist data using localStorage

  • 🧾 Export data as CSV or JSON

  • 🌙 Dark/Light mode toggle (optional bonus)

💻 Tech Stack

  • React + TailwindCSS (UI)

  • Recharts / Chart.js (Visualization)

  • Context API / useReducer (State management)

  • LocalStorage API (Persistence)

🧠 Learning Goals

  • Managing complex state in React

  • Using chart libraries for visualization

  • Handling form inputs and dynamic lists

  • Data filtering and transformation

  • Persisting data locally

🧪 Sample Test Cases

Input | Expected Output -- | -- Add expense “Food – ₹250” | Shows in expense list under Food Filter by “Travel” | Only travel-related expenses appear Add 3 categories | Pie chart updates dynamically Reload page | Data persists via localStorage

📦 Acceptance Criteria

  • Folder: projects/finance-dashboard

  • Must include README.md with setup, features, and screenshots

  • At least 3 screenshots (Home, Add Expense, Analytics View)

  • Responsive UI (mobile + desktop)

  • Uses React best practices (components, hooks, clean structure)

issues.title: WAP to detect Cycle in a Linked List
issues.body:
# Add Cycle Detection in Linked List

## Problem Description

Detect if a linked list contains a cycle using Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm (Tortoise and Hare).

**Input:** Head of a linked list  
**Output:** `true` if cycle exists, `false` otherwise

**Example:**
```
Input: head = [3,2,0,-4], pos = 1
Output: true (tail connects back to node at index 1)

Input: head = [1,2], pos = -1
Output: false (no cycle)
```

## Algorithm

Uses two pointers moving at different speeds:
- **Slow pointer:** moves 1 step at a time
- **Fast pointer:** moves 2 steps at a time
- If they meet, a cycle exists
- If fast reaches null, no cycle

**Time Complexity:** O(n)  
**Space Complexity:** O(1)

## Why Add This?

1. Currently linkedlist folder only has `reverseLinkedList.js` - this adds essential variety
2. Floyd's Algorithm is a must-know pattern for linked list problems
3. Demonstrates two-pointer technique with different speeds (not covered elsewhere)
4. Very common in technical interviews

## Proposed Location

```
dsa/linkedlist/detectCycle.js
```

issues.title: Add “Kadane’s Algorithm for Maximum Subarray Sum”
issues.body:
Add a new file kadaneAlgorithm.js implementing Kadane’s Algorithm to find the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray.
Include both the standard version and one that returns the subarray itself.
